1371	\begin{proof}
1372	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1373	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1374	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1375	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1376	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1377	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1378	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1379	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1380	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1381	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1382	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1383	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1384	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1385	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1386	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1387	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1388	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1389	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1390	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1391	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1392	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1393	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1394	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1395	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1396	
1397	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1398	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1399	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1400	
1401	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1402	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1403	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1404	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1405	  \begin{enumerate}
1406	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1407	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1408	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1409	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1410	      \(P_d\)-modules
1411	      \[
1412	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1413	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1414	      \]
1415	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1416	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1417	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1418	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1419	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1420	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1421	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1422	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1423	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1424	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1425	      to \(A_g\).
1426	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1427	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1428	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1429	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1430	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1431	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1432	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1433	      over \(A_f\).
1434	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1435	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1436	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1437	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1438	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1439	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1440	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1441	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1442	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1443	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1444	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1445	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1446	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1447	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1448	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1449	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1450	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1451	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1452	      completes the induction.
1453	  \end{enumerate}
1454	\end{proof}
1455	
1456	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1457	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1458	
1459	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1460	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1461	
1462	\begin{lemma}
1463	
1464	
1465	
1466	\leanok
1467	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1468	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1470	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1471	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1472	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1473	\end{lemma}
1474	\begin{proof}
1475	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1476	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1477	\end{proof}
1478	
1479	\begin{lemma}
1480	
1481	
1482	
1483	\leanok
1484	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1485	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1487	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1488	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1489	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1490	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1491	  is free over \(A_f\).
1492	\end{lemma}
1493	\begin{proof}
1494	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1495	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1496	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1497	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1498	\end{proof}

1500	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1501	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1502	
1503	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1507	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1508	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1512	
1513	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1514	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1516	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1517	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1518	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1519	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1520	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1521	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1522	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1523	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1524	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1525	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1526	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1527	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1528	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1529	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1530	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1531	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1532	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1533	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1534	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1535	\end{lemma}
1536	
1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1539	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1540	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1541	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1542	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1543	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
1544	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1545	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1546	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
1547	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
1548	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
1549	  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
1550	  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
1551	  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1552	  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
1553	  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
1554	  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
1555	  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
1556	  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
1557	  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1558	\end{proof}
1559	
1560	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1561	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1563	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1564	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1565	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1566	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1567	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1568	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1569	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1570	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1571	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1572	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1573	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1574	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1575	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1576	\end{lemma}
1577	
1578	\begin{proof}
1579	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1580	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1581	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1582	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1583	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1584	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1585	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1586	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1587	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1588	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1589	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1590	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1591	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1592	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1593	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1594	\end{proof}
1595	
1596	
1597	\begin{theorem}
1598	
1599	
1600	
1601	\leanok
1602	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1603	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1604	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1605	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1606	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1607	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1608	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1609	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1610	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1611	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1612	  %
1613	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1614	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1615	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1616	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1617	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1618	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1619	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1620	  %
1621	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (matches the landed .lean file):
1622	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1623	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] [QuasiCompact p] (F : X.Modules)
1624	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1625	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1626	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1627	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1628	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1629	  %
1630	  % NOTE (iter-023): `[QuasiCompact p]` is essential — LocallyOfFiniteType does NOT
1631	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
1632	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
1633	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
1634	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
1635	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).
1636	
1637	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1638	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1639	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1640	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1641	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1642	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1643	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1644	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1645	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1646	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1647	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1648	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1649	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1650	
1651	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1652	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1653	\end{theorem}
1654	
1655	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1656	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1657	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1658	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1659	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1660	% by restricting to a non-empty
1661	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1662	\begin{proof}
1663	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1664	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1665	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1666	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1667	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1668	
1669	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1670	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1671	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1672	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1673	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1674	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1675	
1676	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1677	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1678	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1679	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1680	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1681	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1682	
1683	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1684	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1685	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1686	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1687	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1688	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1689	
1690	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1691	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1692	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1693	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1694	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1695	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1696	\end{proof}

1698	\section{Mathlib status}
1699	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1700	
1701	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1702	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1703	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1704	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1705	generic-flatness argument consumes
1706	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1707	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1708	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1709	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1710	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1711	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1712	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1713	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1714	are introduced in
1715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

1116	  Since \(P^I_I = 1\) is a unit (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_self}), the structure map
1117	  \(R^I \to R^I[1/P^I_I]\) is the localisation at a unit, hence an isomorphism of
1118	  rings; applying \(\Spec\) gives an isomorphism of schemes. Proved directly in
1119	  Lean.
1120	\end{proof}
1121	
1122	\begin{definition}
1123	
1124	\leanok
1125	[The scheme-level transition \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\)]
1126	  \label{def:gr_chart_transition}
1127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition}
1128	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1129	  The scheme-level transition is the \(\Spec\) of the ring transition map
1130	  \(\theta_{I,J} : R^J[1/P^J_I] \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}):
1131	  \[
1132	    U^I_J \;=\; \Spec R^I[1/P^I_J] \longrightarrow \Spec R^J[1/P^J_I] \;=\; U^J_I .
1133	  \]
1134	  It is the \(t\)-field of the Grassmannian glue datum.
1135	\end{definition}
1136	
1137	\begin{lemma}
1138	
1139	\leanok
1140	[The self-transition is the identity]
1141	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}
1142	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition_self}
1143	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition, lem:gr_transition_self}
1144	  For every \(I\), the scheme-level self-transition
1145	  \(U^I_I \to U^I_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}) is the identity. This is
1146	  the \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\)-field of the glue datum.
1147	\end{lemma}
1148	\begin{proof}
1149	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition, lem:gr_transition_self}
1150	  \leanok
1151	  By \cref{lem:gr_transition_self} the ring map \(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity,
1152	  and \(\Spec\) sends the identity ring map to the identity morphism. Proved
1153	  directly in Lean.
1154	\end{proof}

1156	\subsection*{The away-localisation pullback isomorphism}
1157	
1158	\begin{definition}
1159	
1160	\leanok
1161	[Pullback of two principal opens of \(\Spec A\)]
1162	  \label{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}
1163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso}
1164	  \uses{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1165	  Let \(A\) be a commutative ring and \(x, y \in A\). The fibre product of the two
1166	  basic-open inclusions \(\Spec A[1/x] \to \Spec A \leftarrow \Spec A[1/y]\) is
1167	  canonically isomorphic to the spectrum of the doubly-inverted localisation:
1168	  \[
1169	    \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1170	      \;\cong\; \Spec A[1/(xy)].
1171	  \]
1172	  The isomorphism is the composite of the tensor-product identification of the
1173	  pullback (\cref{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso}) with the \(\Spec\) of the
1174	  localisation isomorphism
1175	  \(A[1/x] \otimes_A A[1/y] \cong A[1/(xy)]\)
1176	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul},
1177	  \cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}). It is stated over a general base
1178	  ring \(A\) so that it may be instantiated at the triple-overlap rings of the
1179	  Grassmannian glue datum.
1180	\end{definition}
1181	
1182	\begin{lemma}
1183	
1184	\leanok
1185	[Left leg of the pullback isomorphism]
1186	  \label{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}
1187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}
1188	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_left}
1189	  Under the identification of \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}, the first
1190	  projection of the pullback corresponds to the left away-inclusion: the
1191	  composite
1192	  \(\Spec A[1/(xy)] \to \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1193	    \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_1} \Spec A[1/x]\)
1194	  equals \(\Spec\) of the inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\)
1195	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}).
1196	\end{lemma}
1197	\begin{proof}
1198	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_left}
1199	  \leanok
1200	  The pullback-projection compatibility of the tensor-product identification
1201	  sends \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) to the left tensor inclusion, which under the
1202	  localisation isomorphism is the structure map \(A[1/x] \to A[1/(xy)]\), i.e.\
1203	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\); the verification is the universal property of the
1204	  away-localisation. Proved directly in Lean.
1205	\end{proof}
1206	
1207	\begin{lemma}
1208	
1209	\leanok
1210	[Right leg of the pullback isomorphism]
1211	  \label{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}
1212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}
1213	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_right}
1214	  Symmetrically, the second projection of the pullback corresponds to the right
1215	  away-inclusion: the composite
1216	  \(\Spec A[1/(xy)] \to \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1217	    \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_2} \Spec A[1/y]\)
1218	  equals \(\Spec\) of \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\)
1219	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}).
1220	\end{lemma}
1221	\begin{proof}
1222	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_incl_right}
1223	  \leanok
1224	  As in \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}, with the right tensor inclusion in
1225	  place of the left. Proved directly in Lean.
1226	\end{proof}
1227	
1228	\begin{definition}
1229	
1230	\leanok
1231	[Order-swap isomorphism \(A[1/(xy)] \cong A[1/(yx)]\)]
1232	  \label{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayMulCommEquiv}
1234	  \uses{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1235	  For \(x, y \in A\), the canonical ring isomorphism
1236	  \[
1237	    A[1/(xy)] \;\cong\; A[1/(yx)]
1238	  \]
1239	  identifying the two away-localisations, which agree because \(xy\) and \(yx\)
1240	  generate the same submonoid of \(A\)
1241	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}). It is inserted into the
1242	  triple-cocycle field of the glue datum to reconcile the opposite product
1243	  orders produced by the two pullback isomorphisms.
1244	\end{definition}
1245	
1246	\begin{lemma}
1247	
1248	\leanok
1249	[The order-swap lies over the base ring]
1250	  \label{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}
1251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}
1252	  \uses{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1253	  The order-swap isomorphism (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}) intertwines the
1254	  two structure maps: for \(x, y \in A\),
1255	  \[
1256	    \mathrm{swap}_{x,y} \circ \bigl(A \to A[1/(xy)]\bigr)
1257	      \;=\; \bigl(A \to A[1/(yx)]\bigr).
1258	  \]
1259	\end{lemma}
1260	\begin{proof}
1261	  \uses{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1262	  \leanok
1263	  Both \(A[1/(xy)]\) and \(A[1/(yx)]\) are localisations of \(A\) at the same
1264	  submonoid (\(xy\) and \(yx\) generate it), and the canonical comparison map
1265	  between two such localisations is an \(A\)-algebra isomorphism
1266	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}); an \(A\)-algebra map commutes with
1267	  the structure maps. Proved directly in Lean.
1268	\end{proof}
1269	
1270	\begin{lemma}
1271	
1272	\leanok
1273	[The order-swap absorbs the left inclusion into the right one]
1274	  \label{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_awayInclLeft}
1275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayMulCommEquiv_comp_awayInclLeft}
1276	  \uses{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_away_incl_right, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1277	  For \(x, y \in A\), pre-composing the order-swap isomorphism
1278	  \(\mathrm{swap}_{x,y} : A[1/(xy)] \cong A[1/(yx)]\)
1279	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}) with the left away-inclusion
1280	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : A[1/x] \to A[1/(xy)]\)
1281	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}) yields the right away-inclusion of the swapped
1282	  pair:
1283	  \[
1284	    \mathrm{swap}_{x,y} \circ \iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}
1285	      \;=\; \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{y,x}
1286	      \;:\; A[1/x] \longrightarrow A[1/(yx)] ,
1287	  \]
1288	  where \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{y,x} : A[1/x] \to A[1/(yx)]\) is the right
1289	  away-inclusion (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}) for the pair \((y, x)\). This is
1290	  the order-swap coherence used to rotate the triple-overlap transitions.
1291	\end{lemma}
1292	\begin{proof}
1293	  \uses{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_away_incl_right, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1294	  \leanok
1295	  Both sides are ring maps out of the away-localisation \(A[1/x]\), so by the
1296	  universal property of the localisation -- the uniqueness of an extension along
1297	  the structure map \(A \to A[1/x]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) -- it suffices
1298	  to check equality after pre-composing with that structure map, i.e.\ on the base
1299	  ring \(A\). There all three away-maps collapse to structure maps into
1300	  \(A[1/(yx)]\): the left inclusion by
1301	  \cref{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}, the order-swap by
1302	  \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}, and the right inclusion by
1303	  \cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}. The two composites therefore agree
1304	  on \(A\), hence everywhere. Proved directly in Lean.
1305	\end{proof}

1307	\subsection*{The triple-overlap glue field \(t'\)}
1308	
1309	The scheme-gluing machine requires, beyond the pairwise transitions \(t_{I,J}\), a
1310	\emph{triple-overlap} field \(t'\) on the relevant fibre products, together with
1311	its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) with the chart projections. We record both
1312	as the next layer of bridge results; their cocycle coherence is the residual
1313	obligation discharged in \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
1314	
1315	\begin{definition}
1316	
1317	\leanok
1318	[The triple-overlap transition \(t'\)]
1319	  \label{def:gr_chart_transition'}
1320	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'}
1321	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1322	  The \(t'\)-field of the Grassmannian glue datum is the morphism of schemes
1323	  \[
1324	    t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \longrightarrow U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1325	  \]
1326	  defined as the composite
1327	  \[
1328	    U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K
1329	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Spec S_I
1330	      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \Theta_{I,J}\ } \Spec S_J
1331	      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \mathrm{swap}\ } \Spec R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)]
1332	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1333	  \]
1334	  where the first and last arrows are the pullback identifications of
1335	  \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso} -- the source one for the pair \((P^I_J, P^I_K)\)
1336	  over \(R^I\), and the target one (taken inverse) for the pair \((P^J_K, P^J_I)\)
1337	  over \(R^J\) -- the middle arrow is the \(\Spec\) of the localised
1338	  triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)
1339	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), and \(\mathrm{swap}\) is the \(\Spec\) of the
1340	  order-swap isomorphism \(R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)] \cong S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\)
1341	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}). The order-swap is inserted precisely to
1342	  reconcile the product order \(P^J_K P^J_I\) carried by the target pullback
1343	  identification with the order \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the codomain \(S_J\) of
1344	  \(\Theta_{I,J}\).
1345	\end{definition}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}
1348	
1349	\leanok
1350	[The \(t'\) ring identity]
1351	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
1352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
1353	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1354	  Over the triple-overlap rings, the localised transition \(\Theta_{I,J}\)
1355	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the order-swap and the right
1356	  inclusion equals the left inclusion post-composed with the plain transition
1357	  \(\theta_{I,J}\), as ring homomorphisms \(R^J[1/P^J_I] \to S_I\):
1358	  \[
1359	    \Theta_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap} \circ \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{P^J_K, P^J_I}
1360	      \;=\; \iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{P^I_J, P^I_K} \circ \theta_{I,J} .
1361	  \]
1362	\end{lemma}
1363	\begin{proof}
1364	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1365	  \leanok
1366	  Both sides are ring maps out of the away-localisation \(R^J[1/P^J_I]\), so by the
1367	  universal property of the localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) it suffices
1368	  to check equality after pre-composing with the structure map
1369	  \(R^J \to R^J[1/P^J_I]\), i.e.\ on the chart ring \(R^J\). There the right
1370	  inclusion and the order-swap collapse to structure maps
1371	  (\cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap},
1372	  \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}), and the lift defining
1373	  \(\Theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) unwinds to
1374	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
1375	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}); the right side likewise unwinds through the lift
1376	  defining \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) to the same
1377	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\). Proved directly in Lean.
1378	\end{proof}
1379	
1380	\begin{lemma}
1381	
1382	\leanok
1383	[The \(t'\) compatibility field \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)]
1384	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}
1385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_fac}
1386	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd, def:gr_chart_transition}
1387	  The triple-overlap transition \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) is
1388	  compatible with the projections to the charts:
1389	  \[
1390	    \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ t'_{I,J,K} \;=\; t_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{pr}_1 ,
1391	  \]
1392	  where \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) is the first projection of the source pullback
1393	  \(U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\), \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) the second projection of the
1394	  target pullback \(U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\), and \(t_{I,J}\) the scheme-level
1395	  transition (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}).
1396	\end{lemma}
1397	\begin{proof}
1398	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd, def:gr_chart_transition}
1399	  \leanok
1400	  Both fibre products are affine, so the identity may be checked after applying
1401	  \(\Spec\), as an identity of the underlying ring homomorphisms. Rewriting the
1402	  source projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) through the pullback identification
1403	  (\cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}) and the target projection
1404	  \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) through \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd} turns the claim
1405	  into the ring identity \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity}: the three
1406	  localised pieces of \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) collapse to the
1407	  left inclusion followed by the plain transition \(t_{I,J}\)
1408	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}). Proved directly in Lean.
1409	\end{proof}
1410	
1411	\begin{lemma}
1412	
1413	\leanok
1414	[The triple-overlap cocycle coherence]
1415	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}
1416	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_cocycle}
1417	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1418	  The triple-overlap transitions \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) satisfy
1419	  the cocycle coherence on the source triple overlap \(U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\):
1420	  \[
1421	    t'_{I,J,K} \;\circ\; t'_{J,K,I} \;\circ\; t'_{K,I,J} \;=\; \mathrm{id}.
1422	  \]
1423	  This is the \(\mathrm{cocycle}\) field of the Grassmannian glue datum -- the
1424	  categorical realisation of the rotated ring identity \(\Phi = \id\)
1425	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}).
1426	\end{lemma}
1427	\begin{proof}
1428	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1429	  \leanok
1430	  Expand each factor \(t'\) into its constituents
1431	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}): a source pullback identification, the
1432	  \(\Spec\) of a localised triple-overlap transition \(\Theta\)
1433	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the \(\Spec\) of an
1434	  order-swap (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), and an inverse target pullback
1435	  identification. In the threefold composite the two internal pairs
1436	  \(\mathrm{ap}^{-1} \circ \mathrm{ap}\) of pullback identifications
1437	  (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) cancel, adjacent factors sharing the same
1438	  identification. There remain the six \(\Spec\)-arrows of the three \(\Theta\)'s
1439	  and three order-swaps; these collapse into a single \(\Spec\) of their composite
1440	  ring endomorphism \(\Phi\). By \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id} one has
1441	  \(\Phi = \id\), so this middle \(\Spec\) is the identity, and the outer
1442	  conjugating pullback isomorphism cancels against its inverse, leaving the
1443	  identity of the source triple overlap. Proved directly in Lean.
1444	\end{proof}
1445	
1446	\begin{lemma}
1447	
1448	\leanok
1449	[Rotation lemma for the triple-overlap transitions]
1450	  \label{lem:gr_rotMid}
1451	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rotMid}
1452	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_awayInclLeft}
1453	  Conjugating the rotated transition \(\Theta_{J,K,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij})
1454	  by the two order-swaps \(\mathrm{swap}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}) recovers the
1455	  \(J,K\)-transition \(\Theta_{J,K}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk}) in the \(I,J,K\) frame:
1456	  \(\mathrm{swap} \circ \Theta_{J,K,I} \circ \mathrm{swap} = \Theta_{J,K}\).
1457	\end{lemma}
1458	\begin{proof}
1459	
1460	\leanok
1461	  Both sides are away-localisation lifts of \(\tilde\theta_{J,K}\); checking on the chart ring
1462	  \(R^K\) reduces to \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap} and
1463	  \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_awayInclLeft}. Proved directly in Lean.
1464	\end{proof}
1465	
1466	\begin{lemma}
1467	
1468	\leanok
1469	[Matrix collapse behind the inverse pair]
1470	  \label{lem:gr_transitionInvImageMatrix}
1471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionInvImageMatrix}
1472	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_minor, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
1473	  Pushing the image matrix \((X^K_I)^{-1} X^K\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) of \(\theta_{K,I}\)
1474	  forward along \(\theta_{I,K}\) — realised as \(\Theta_{I,K}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik})
1475	  post-composed with the left inclusion (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}) — recovers the universal
1476	  matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) over the triple-overlap ring \(S_I\).
1477	\end{lemma}
1478	\begin{proof}
1479	
1480	\leanok
1481	  Both sides reduce to \(W = X^I\) over \(S_I\) via the \((W_K)^{-1} W\) collapse, using
1482	  \(W_I = 1\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), naturality of the image matrix
1483	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}) and of the Cramer inverse (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}),
1484	  and the unit \(\det(W_K)\) (\cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}). Proved directly in Lean.
1485	\end{proof}
1486	
1487	\begin{lemma}
1488	
1489	\leanok
1490	[The residual inverse pair \(\theta_{I,K} \circ \theta_{K,I} = \id\)]
1491	  \label{lem:gr_transitionInvPair}
1492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionInvPair}
1493	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionInvImageMatrix, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_universal_matrix, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}
1494	  Over the triple-overlap ring \(S_I = R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]\) the inverse pair, phrased through the
1495	  localised transitions and the order-swap, is the identity:
1496	  \(\Theta_{I,J,K} \circ \Theta_{K,I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap}_I = \id\)
1497	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik}, \cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij},
1498	  \cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}).
1499	\end{lemma}
1500	\begin{proof}
1501	
1502	\leanok
1503	  By \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.ringHom\_ext}\) it suffices to check on the chart-ring generators of
1504	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}); there the matrix collapse
1505	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionInvImageMatrix} closes each generator. Proved directly in Lean.
1506	\end{proof}
1507	
1508	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1509	[The rotated triple-overlap ring cocycle identity \(\Phi = \id\)]
1510	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}
1511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocyclePhiId}
1512	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
1513	    lem:gr_cocycle}
1514	  \textit{The ring identity underlying the \(\mathrm{cocycle}\) field of the glue datum.}
1515	  Fix \(d \le r\) and three subsets \(I,J,K \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality \(d\). On the
1516	  triple-overlap chart ring \(R_{IJK} := \mathrm{Localization.Away}\,(P^I_J\cdot P^I_K)\)
1517	  the composite ring endomorphism
1518	  \[
1519	    \Phi := \Theta_{I,J,K} \circ \mathrm{swap}_J \circ \Theta_{J,K,I} \circ \mathrm{swap}_K
1520	            \circ \Theta_{K,I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap}_I
1521	  \]
1522	  equals the identity \(\mathrm{RingHom.id}\,R_{IJK}\), where each \(\Theta_{X,Y,Z} =
1523	  \mathtt{cocycleΘIJ}\,d\,r\,X\,Y\,Z\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}, taken for the rotated index
1524	  triple) and each \(\mathrm{swap}_X\) is the matching order-swap algebra equivalence
1525	  \(\mathtt{awayMulCommEquiv}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}).
1526	\end{lemma}
1527	\begin{proof}
1528	  \uses{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq, lem:gr_cocycle}
1529	  \leanok
1530	  Both endomorphisms are ring homomorphisms out of the localization
1531	  \(R_{IJK} = \mathrm{Localization.Away}\,(P^I_J\cdot P^I_K)\), so by Mathlib's
1532	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.ringHom\_ext}\) for the submonoid generated by
1533	  \(P^I_J\cdot P^I_K\) it suffices to
1534	  check equality on the chart-ring generators (the images of the universal matrix entries). On those
1535	  generators \(\Phi\) unwinds, via the order-swaps and the \(\Theta\) definitions, to the rotated
1536	  analogue of the matrix cocycle collapse \((Y_K)^{-1}Y\) already proved in
1537	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq} — the same image-matrix identity that underlies the scheme-level
1538	  cocycle condition \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}. Reusing that computation for the rotated triple shows each
1539	  generator is fixed, whence \(\Phi = \id\).
1540	\end{proof}

1542	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1543	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1544	
1545	\begin{definition}
1546	
1547	\leanok
1548	[The Grassmannian glue datum]
1549	  \label{def:gr_the_glue_data}
1550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.theGlueData}
1551	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_chart_overlap, def:gr_chart_incl,
1552	    def:gr_chart_transition, def:gr_chart_transition',
1553	    lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion, lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso,
1554	    lem:gr_chartTransition_self, lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac,
1555	    lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}
1556	  The \emph{Grassmannian glue datum} is the \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) bundle
1557	  indexed by the set \(\{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of
1558	  size-\(d\) subsets, assembling the data constructed above:
1559	  \begin{itemize}
1560	    \item objects \(U := U^I\), the affine charts (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart});
1561	    \item overlaps \(V := U^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap});
1562	    \item inclusions \(f := \bigl(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\bigr)\)
1563	      (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}), which are open immersions
1564	      (\cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}), with the self-inclusion an
1565	      isomorphism (the \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\) field,
1566	      \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso});
1567	    \item transitions \(t := t_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}), with
1568	      \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\) field \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self};
1569	    \item the triple-overlap field \(t' := t'_{I,J,K}\)
1570	      (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}), with compatibility field
1571	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}) and \(\mathrm{cocycle}\)
1572	      field (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}).
1573	  \end{itemize}
1574	  The remaining structural fields \(f_{\mathrm{mono}}\) (each inclusion is a
1575	  monomorphism) and \(f_{\mathrm{hasPullback}}\) (the relevant fibre products
1576	  exist) are discharged by instance synthesis from the open-immersion property of
1577	  the inclusions. Assembling these named fields into the
1578	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) record yields the datum from which the glued scheme
1579	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is formed.
1580	\end{definition}
1581	
1582	\begin{definition}
1583	
1584	\leanok
1585	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1586	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1587	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1588	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle,
1589	    def:gr_chart_overlap, def:gr_chart_incl, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion,
1590	    lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso, def:gr_chart_transition,
1591	    lem:gr_chartTransition_self, def:gr_away_pullback_iso,
1592	    lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd,
1593	    def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij,
1594	    def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac,
1595	    lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
1596	    lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}
1597	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1598	  % affine patches" (read from
1599	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1600	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1601	  % "Therefore the schemes
1602	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1603	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1604	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1605	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1606	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1607	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1608	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1609	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1610	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1611	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1612	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1613	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1614	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1615	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1616	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1617	  \[
1618	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1619	  \]
1620	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1621	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1622	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1623	
1624	  \emph{Construction of the glue datum.} The gluing is carried out through the
1625	  Mathlib vehicle for scheme gluing, a glue datum in the sense of a category of
1626	  schemes (the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\), extending the abstract
1627	  glue-datum interface). Its data are supplied as follows. The index set is the
1628	  set \(J := \{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\)
1629	  subsets. The objects are the charts \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}); the
1630	  pairwise overlaps are the schemes \(U^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}); the
1631	  inclusions \(f_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^I\) are the open immersions
1632	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}\) (open immersions by
1633	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}, in particular monomorphisms), with the
1634	  identity field \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\) supplied by
1635	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso}; the transition isomorphisms
1636	  \(t_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^J_I\) are the scheme-level transitions
1637	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}\), with identity field \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\)
1638	  supplied by \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}.
1639	
1640	  The triple-overlap field \(t'\) and its first coherence obligation are supplied
1641	  by the two preceding results. The field itself is the morphism
1642	  \(t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \to U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\) of
1643	  \cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}, and the compatibility field
1644	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) -- that \(t'\) is compatible with the projections to the
1645	  charts -- is \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}, which reduces (both fibre
1646	  products being affine) to the ring identity
1647	  \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity} via the leg lemmas
1648	  \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}.
1649	
1650	  \emph{Cocycle field.} The \(t'\)-cocycle coherence
1651	  \(t'_{I,J,K} \circ t'_{J,K,I} \circ t'_{K,I,J} = \mathrm{id}\)
1652	  follows from \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle} via the ring identity
1653	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}. With this field in hand the glue datum is
1654	  complete: it is the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) with
1655	  \(U := \texttt{affineChart}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}),
1656	  \(V := \texttt{chartOverlap}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}),
1657	  \(f := \texttt{chartIncl}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}),
1658	  \(t := \texttt{chartTransition}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}),
1659	  \(t_{\mathrm{id}} := \texttt{chartTransition\_self}\)
1660	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}),
1661	  \(t' := \texttt{chartTransition'}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}),
1662	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}} := \texttt{chartTransition'\_fac}\)
1663	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}), and the cocycle field above. The glued
1664	  scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is then the colimit
1665	  \((\text{glue datum}).\mathrm{glued}\) produced by the gluing machine.
1666	
1667	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1668	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1669	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1670	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1671	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1672	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1673	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1674	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1675	  follows.
1676	\end{definition}
1677	
1678	\section{Separatedness}
1679	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1680	
1681	The surjectivity of the restricted-diagonal comorphism is the ring-theoretic
1682	heart of separatedness. We isolate it through the following sequence of
1683	helper results before stating the keystone lemma.
1684	
1685	\begin{lemma}
1686	
1687	\leanok
1688	[Two-sided inverse identity \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\,P^I_J = 1\)]
1689	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1690	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1691	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
1692	  In \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), the pre-localisation transition hom
1693	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) satisfies the explicit
1694	  two-sided inverse identity
1695	  \[
1696	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_I\bigr) \cdot P^I_J \;=\; 1 .
1697	  \]
1698	  This refines the unit assertion of \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} to a named
1699	  inverse: \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) is the explicit inverse of \(P^I_J\) in
1700	  \(R^I_J\).
1701	\end{lemma}
1702	\begin{proof}
1703	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
1704	  \leanok
1705	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet} (taking the third subset \(K = I\)),
1706	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) is the determinant of the \(I\)-minor of the image
1707	  matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), which by
1708	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap} equals
1709	  \(\det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\). Hence
1710	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\), and multiplying by \(P^I_J =
1711	  \det(X^I_J)\) gives \(\det(X^I_J)^{-1} \cdot \det(X^I_J) = 1\) by the
1712	  localisation-inverse cancellation for the unit \(\det(X^I_J)\)
1713	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}).
1714	\end{proof}
1715	
1716	\begin{definition}
1717	
1718	\leanok
1719	[Restricted-diagonal comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1720	  \label{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1721	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap}
1722	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre}
1723	  The \emph{restricted-diagonal comorphism} on the patch \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1724	  U^J\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism
1725	  \[
1726	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1727	    \longrightarrow R^I_J,
1728	    \qquad
1729	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad
1730	    1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1731	  \]
1732	  whose surjectivity is the content of separatedness; this is the \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1733	  of \cref{lem:gr_separated}.
1734	\end{definition}
1735	\begin{proof}
1736	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre}
1737	  \leanok
1738	  Construct \(\delta_{I,J}\) as the tensor-product lift of the two commuting
1739	  factors: the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = R^I \to R^I_J\) on the left
1740	  factor, and the pre-localisation transition hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
1741	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) on the right factor. Since both land in the
1742	  commutative ring \(R^I_J\), the universal property of the tensor product of
1743	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras yields a unique \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out
1744	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) restricting to these
1745	  factors; by construction its right component is \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\).
1746	\end{proof}
1747	
1748	\begin{lemma}
1749	
1750	\leanok
1751	[Left component of \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1752	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left}
1753	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_left}
1754	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1755	  For \(a \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1756	  (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) acts on the left factor by the structure map:
1757	  \[
1758	    \delta_{I,J}(a \otimes 1) \;=\; \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a),
1759	  \]
1760	  the image of \(a\) under \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = R^I \to R^I_J\).
1761	\end{lemma}
1762	\begin{proof}
1763	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1764	  \leanok
1765	  Immediate from the defining tensor-product lift of
1766	  \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}: the value on a left elementary tensor \(a \otimes
1767	  1\) is the left factor evaluated at \(a\), i.e.\ the structure map.
1768	\end{proof}
1769	
1770	\begin{lemma}
1771	
1772	\leanok
1773	[Right component of \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1774	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right}
1775	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_right}
1776	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1777	  For \(b \in \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\), the comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1778	  (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) acts on the right factor by the pre-localisation
1779	  transition hom:
1780	  \[
1781	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes b) \;=\; \tilde\theta_{I,J}(b)
1782	  \]
1783	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}).
1784	\end{lemma}
1785	\begin{proof}
1786	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1787	  \leanok
1788	  Immediate from the defining tensor-product lift of
1789	  \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}: the value on a right elementary tensor \(1 \otimes
1790	  b\) is the right factor \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) evaluated at \(b\).
1791	\end{proof}
1792	
1793	\begin{lemma}
1794	
1795	\leanok
1796	[The restricted-diagonal comorphism is surjective]
1797	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective}
1798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_surjective}
1799	  \uses{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1800	  The comorphism \(\delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
1801	  \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) is surjective.
1802	\end{lemma}
1803	\begin{proof}
1804	  \uses{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1805	  \leanok
1806	  Let \(z \in R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) is the
1807	  localisation of \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) away from \(P^I_J\), surjectivity of
1808	  the localisation map provides \(a \in R^I\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) with
1809	  \[
1810	    z \cdot (P^I_J)^n \;=\; \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a).
1811	  \]
1812	  Consider the witness \(a \otimes (P^J_I)^n \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]
1813	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\). Using multiplicativity together with
1814	  \cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left} and \cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right},
1815	  \[
1816	    \delta_{I,J}\bigl(a \otimes (P^J_I)^n\bigr)
1817	      = \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a) \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)^n
1818	      = z \cdot (P^I_J)^n \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)^n
1819	      = z \cdot \bigl(P^I_J \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\bigr)^n
1820	      = z,
1821	  \]
1822	  where the last equality uses \(P^I_J \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1\) from
1823	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}. Hence \(z\) lies in the image
1824	  of \(\delta_{I,J}\), so \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective.
1825	\end{proof}
1826	
1827	\begin{definition}
1828	
1829	\leanok
1830	[Source pullback iso \(e_2\) for the diagonal computation]
1831	  \label{def:gr_pullbackιIso}
1832	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.pullbackιIso}
1833	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1834	  The \emph{source pullback iso} \(e_2\) identifies the categorical fibre product
1835	  of two chart inclusions of the glued scheme (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) with
1836	  the overlap chart:
1837	  \[
1838	    e_2 : \mathrm{pullback}\,(\iota_i)\,(\iota_j) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1839	    U^I \cap U^J \;=\; \texttt{chartOverlap}\,(d,r,i,j)

3866	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the

3868	  Consider the cartesian square
3869	  \[
3870	    \begin{array}{ccc}

3872	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]

3874	    \end{array}

3877	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
3878	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
3879	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3880	  \[
3881	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3882	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
3883	  \]

3885	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
3886	  \[

3889	  \]

3891	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
3892	  \(\mathcal{F}\).

4012	\subsection{The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{eqLocus}$}{eqLocus} globalization (FBC-B)}
4013	
4014	The separated-case argument is repackaged here at the level of \(A\)-modules, where
4015	\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf, so that flat
4016	base change becomes the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
4017	(Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) applied to an honest
4018	\(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\). The blocks below construct that presentation concretely: every
4019	module of sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\) is viewed as an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along the
4020	structure-sheaf restriction \(A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), the restriction maps of \(M\) become
4021	\(A\)-linear, and the two product legs of the sheaf condition assemble into a pair of \(A\)-linear maps
4022	whose equalizer locus is \(\Gamma(X, M)\). Throughout, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is a sheaf of
4023	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) is an open cover with
4024	\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).
4025	
4026	The bijectivity of the keystone presentation rests on two element-level sheaf axioms of the underlying
4027	abelian-group sheaf \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), recorded as Mathlib dependency anchors.
4028	
4029	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf separatedness: local equality forces global equality]
4030	  \label{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
4031	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'}
4032	  \mathlibok
4033	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4034	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on a topological space \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a
4035	  family covering an open \(V\) (i.e.\ \(V \le \bigvee_i U_i\)), and \(s, t \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) two
4036	  sections. If the restrictions of \(s\) and \(t\) to every \(U_i\) agree, then \(s = t\). This is the
4037	  separatedness half of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
4038	\end{lemma}
4039	
4040	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf gluing: a compatible family has a unique amalgamation]
4041	  \label{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4042	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'}
4043	  \mathlibok
4044	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4045	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a family covering an open
4046	  \(V\), and \((s_i)_i\) a family of sections \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) that is \emph{compatible}
4047	  (the restrictions of \(s_i\) and \(s_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree for all \(i, j\)). Then there is a
4048	  unique \(s \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(s_i\). This is the gluing half
4049	  of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
4050	\end{lemma}

4052	\begin{definition}\leanok
4053	[The ground ring of global sections]
4054	  \label{def:fbcb_groundRing}
4055	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.groundRing}
4056	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{ground ring} is the commutative ring
4057	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = \mathcal{O}_X(\top)\) of global sections of the structure sheaf,
4058	  taken from the \(\mathbf{CommRing}\)-valued structure presheaf so its ring and algebra structures
4059	  resolve canonically.

4065	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU}
4066	  \uses{def:fbcb_groundRing}
4067	  For an open \(U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction along \(U \subseteq \top\) is a ring
4068	  homomorphism \(\rho_U : A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the ring map
4069	  along which sections over \(U\) are regarded as \(A\)-modules.

4079	  \[

4085	    along \(V \subseteq \top\), factored as \(V \subseteq U \subseteq \top\); functoriality of the

4111	\begin{definition}\leanok
4112	[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction map]
4113	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA}
4115	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom}
4116	  The underlying \(A\)-linear map \(\Gamma(U,M) \to_A \Gamma(V,M)\) of the \(A\)-module morphism of
4117	  Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom}. It is the building block of the two product legs of the
4118	  equalizer.
4119	\end{definition}
4120	
4121	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4122	[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction is the underlying presheaf restriction]
4123	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
4124	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA_apply}
4125	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4126	  On underlying elements, the \(A\)-linear restriction map of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA} acts
4127	  as the presheaf restriction of \(M\): for \(x \in \Gamma(U,M)\) and \(V \le U\),
4128	  \(\mathrm{gammaResA}(x) = (M.\mathrm{map})\,x\).
4129	  \begin{proof}
4130	    \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4131	    Restriction of scalars and the composition with the comparison isomorphism of
4132	    Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom} leave the underlying function unchanged, so the map agrees
4133	    with \(M.\mathrm{map}\) on elements.
4134	  \end{proof}
4135	\end{lemma}
4136	
4137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4138	[Functoriality of the \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction maps]
4139	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
4140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA_comp}
4141	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
4142	  For opens \(W \le V \le U\) and \(x \in \Gamma(U,M)\), the \(A\)-linear restrictions compose as the
4143	  single restriction:
4144	  \[
4145	    \mathrm{gammaResA}_{V \le U}\!\bigl(x\bigr) \text{ then } \mathrm{gammaResA}_{W \le V}
4146	      \;=\; \mathrm{gammaResA}_{W \le U}\!\bigl(x\bigr).
4147	  \]
4148	  \begin{proof}
4149	    \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
4150	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply} each \(A\)-linear restriction is the underlying presheaf
4151	    restriction of \(M\); functoriality of the presheaf \(M\) collapses the composite of the two
4152	    restrictions \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) to the single restriction \(W \subseteq U\).
4153	  \end{proof}
4154	\end{lemma}
4155	
4156	\begin{definition}\leanok
4157	[The left restriction leg]
4158	  \label{def:fbcb_leftRes}
4159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.leftRes}
4160	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4161	  The \(A\)-linear map
4162	  \[
4163	    \mathrm{leftRes} : \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M) \longrightarrow \prod_{(i,j)} \Gamma(U_i \cap U_j, M),
4164	  \]
4165	  whose \((i,j)\)-component is the \(A\)-linear restriction \(\Gamma(U_i, M) \to \Gamma(U_i \cap U_j, M)\)
4166	  of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA} applied to the \(i\)-th factor (restriction along
4167	  \(U_i \cap U_j \subseteq U_i\)).
4168	\end{definition}
4169	
4170	\begin{definition}\leanok
4171	[The right restriction leg]
4172	  \label{def:fbcb_rightRes}
4173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rightRes}
4174	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4175	  The \(A\)-linear map \(\mathrm{rightRes} : \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M) \to \prod_{(i,j)} \Gamma(U_i \cap U_j, M)\)
4176	  whose \((i,j)\)-component is the \(A\)-linear restriction of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA} applied
4177	  to the \(j\)-th factor (restriction along \(U_i \cap U_j \subseteq U_j\)).
4178	\end{definition}
4179	
4180	\begin{definition}\leanok
4181	[Restriction of a global section to the cover]
4182	  \label{def:fbcb_toCover}
4183	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toCover}
4184	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4185	  The \(A\)-linear map \(\mathrm{toCover} : \Gamma(\top, M) \to \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M)\) restricting a
4186	  global section to each member of the cover, with \(i\)-th component the \(A\)-linear restriction
4187	  \(\Gamma(\top, M) \to \Gamma(U_i, M)\) of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA}.
4188	\end{definition}
4189	
4190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4191	[The restricted family is compatible]
4192	  \label{lem:fbcb_leftRes_toCover}
4193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.leftRes_toCover}
4194	  \uses{def:fbcb_leftRes, def:fbcb_rightRes, def:fbcb_toCover, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
4195	  For every global section \(s \in \Gamma(\top, M)\) the two legs agree on the restricted family:
4196	  \[
4197	    \mathrm{leftRes}\bigl(\mathrm{toCover}(s)\bigr) = \mathrm{rightRes}\bigl(\mathrm{toCover}(s)\bigr).
4198	  \]
4199	  \begin{proof}
4200	    \uses{def:fbcb_leftRes, def:fbcb_rightRes, def:fbcb_toCover, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
4201	    Both sides are, in the \((i,j)\)-component, the restriction of \(s\) from \(\top\) to
4202	    \(U_i \cap U_j\): the left leg restricts \(\top \to U_i \to U_i \cap U_j\) and the right leg
4203	    restricts \(\top \to U_j \to U_i \cap U_j\), and by the functoriality
4204	    Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp} each two-step restriction collapses to the one-step restriction
4205	    \(\top \to U_i \cap U_j\). Hence the two components coincide.
4206	  \end{proof}
4207	\end{lemma}
4208	
4209	\begin{definition}\leanok
4210	[The corestriction into the equalizer locus]
4211	  \label{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus}
4212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toCoverEqLocus}
4213	  \uses{def:fbcb_toCover, lem:fbcb_leftRes_toCover}
4214	  By the compatibility Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_leftRes_toCover}, the map \(\mathrm{toCover}\) corestricts to
4215	  an \(A\)-linear map
4216	  \[
4217	    \mathrm{toCoverEqLocus} : \Gamma(\top, M)
4218	      \longrightarrow \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes}, \mathrm{rightRes})
4219	      = \{\, t \in \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i,M) : \mathrm{leftRes}(t) = \mathrm{rightRes}(t)\,\}.
4220	  \]
4221	\end{definition}
4222	
4223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4224	[Global sections as an \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module equalizer locus]
4225	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus}
4226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaTopEquivEqLocus}
4227	  \uses{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus, def:fbcb_gammaModA, def:fbcb_gammaResA, def:fbcb_leftRes,
4228	        def:fbcb_rightRes, lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4229	  % NOTE: bijectivity is established by the ELEMENT-LEVEL sheaf axioms
4230	  % (lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib for injectivity, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib for
4231	  % surjectivity) on the underlying Ab-sheaf M.presheaf; it does NOT route through the
4232	  % sheaf-condition-fork limit (lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib / gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork).
4233	  For \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and any open cover \(U\) of \(X\) (\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\)), the
4234	  corestriction \(\mathrm{toCoverEqLocus}\) of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus} is an
4235	  \(A\)-linear isomorphism
4236	  \[
4237	    \Gamma(X, M) = \Gamma(\top, M) \;\cong\; \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes}, \mathrm{rightRes}),
4238	  \]
4239	  identifying the global sections, as a module over the ground ring \(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\), with
4240	  the submodule on which the two restriction legs agree.
4241	  \begin{proof}
4242	    \uses{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus, lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib,
4243	          lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4244	    The map \(\mathrm{toCoverEqLocus}\) is \(A\)-linear by construction, so it suffices to prove it
4245	    bijective on underlying sets. \emph{Injectivity:} if two global sections \(s, t\) have equal images,
4246	    their restrictions to every \(U_i\) agree, so by sheaf separatedness
4247	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) applied to the underlying abelian-group sheaf
4248	    \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), \(s = t\). \emph{Surjectivity:} an element of the locus is a family
4249	    \((s_i)_i\) with \(\mathrm{leftRes} = \mathrm{rightRes}\), i.e.\ a compatible family on the cover; by
4250	    the gluing axiom (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) it amalgamates to a global
4251	    section restricting to \((s_i)_i\). Hence \(\mathrm{toCoverEqLocus}\) is an \(A\)-linear bijection,
4252	    so an isomorphism.
4253	  \end{proof}
4254	\end{lemma}
4255	
4256	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4257	[Flat base change commutes with the \texorpdfstring{$H^0$}{H0} equalizer]
4258	  \label{lem:fbcb_baseChangeGammaEquiv}
4259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.baseChangeGammaEquiv}
4260	  \uses{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
4261	  Let \(B\) be a flat \(A\)-algebra (\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)). Then base changing the global
4262	  sections along \(A \to B\) is the equalizer of the base-changed restriction legs: there is a
4263	  \(B\)-linear isomorphism
4264	  \[
4265	    B \otimes_A \Gamma(X, M) \;\cong\;
4266	      \operatorname{eqLocus}\bigl(\mathrm{id}_B \otimes \mathrm{leftRes},\ \mathrm{id}_B \otimes \mathrm{rightRes}\bigr).
4267	  \]
4268	  \begin{proof}
4269	    \uses{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
4270	    Tensoring the keystone isomorphism Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus} by \(B\) over \(A\)
4271	    gives \(B \otimes_A \Gamma(X,M) \cong B \otimes_A \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes},\mathrm{rightRes})\),
4272	    and by flatness of \(B\) over \(A\) the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
4273	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) carries \(B \otimes_A (-)\) of the equalizer to the
4274	    equalizer of the base-changed legs. Composing the two yields the stated \(B\)-linear isomorphism.
4275	    By the same presentation applied to the pulled-back sheaf, the right-hand side is the global sections
4276	    of \(M\) over the base-changed scheme, so this is the module-level core of the \(H^0\)
4277	    flat-base-change comparison.
4278	  \end{proof}
4279	\end{lemma}

1950	\begin{lemma}
1951	
1952	\leanok
1953	[The transition-then-inclusion composite is affine]
1954	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1956	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition, def:gr_chart_incl, def:gr_transition_pre}
1957	  The composite of the scheme-level transition
1958	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}) with the opposite chart inclusion
1959	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}) is the \(\Spec\) of the pre-localisation transition
1960	  homomorphism \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : R^J \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\)
1961	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}):
1962	  \[
1963	    t_{I,J} \circ \iota^J_I \;=\; \Spec \tilde\theta_{I,J} :
1964	      U^I_J \to U^J .
1965	  \]
1966	\end{lemma}
1967	\begin{proof}
1968	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition, def:gr_chart_incl, def:gr_transition_pre}
1969	  \leanok
1970	  Unfolding the two scheme maps, the composite is \(\Spec\) of the ring map
1971	  obtained by precomposing the localised transition \(\theta_{I,J} :
1972	  R^J[1/P^J_I] \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\) with the localisation \(R^J \to R^J[1/P^J_I]\).
1973	  By the universal property of the away-localisation (the lift commutes with the
1974	  localisation map), this composite ring map is exactly the pre-localisation
1975	  transition \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\); applying \(\Spec\) and the contravariance of
1976	  composition gives the claim.
1977	\end{proof}

1979	\begin{lemma}
1980	
1981	\leanok
1982	[The structure morphism is separated (morphism form)]
1983	  \label{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
1984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparatedToSpecZ}
1985	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_pullbackιIso,
1986	    def:gr_diagonalRingMap, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective,
1987	    lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ, lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst,
1988	    lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1989	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1990	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is separated: its diagonal is a closed immersion.
1991	\end{lemma}
1992	\begin{proof}
1993	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_pullbackιIso,
1994	    def:gr_diagonalRingMap, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective,
1995	    lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ, lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst,
1996	    lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1997	    \leanok
1998	  Separatedness is local on the target of the diagonal, so it may be checked on
1999	  the cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) by the
2000	  patch products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) (the left--right product of the
2001	  chart open cover with itself). Fix a patch \((i,j)\). On it the restricted
2002	  diagonal is identified, via the source pullback iso \(e_2\)
2003	  (\cref{def:gr_pullbackιIso}) — whose two legs are computed in
2004	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd},
2005	  using \cref{lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ} and \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
2006	  to read off the affine comorphisms — and the target affine iso (the pullback of
2007	  two affine schemes is the \(\Spec\) of the tensor product), with the affine
2008	  morphism \(\Spec \delta_{I,J}\), where \(\delta_{I,J}\) is the
2009	  restricted-diagonal comorphism of \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}. Since
2010	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective (\cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective}), the
2011	  morphism \(\Spec \delta_{I,J}\) is a closed immersion. As every patch of the
2012	  diagonal is a closed immersion, the diagonal is a closed immersion and the
2013	  structure morphism is separated.
2014	\end{proof}
