2016	\begin{lemma}
2017	
2018	\leanok
2019	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
2020	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
2021	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
2022	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2023	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
2024	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
2025	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2026	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
2027	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
2028	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
2029	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
2030	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2031	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2032	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
2033	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
2034	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
2035	\end{lemma}
2036	
2037	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
2038	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
2039	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2040	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
2041	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
2042	\begin{proof}
2043	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2044	  \leanok
2045	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
2046	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2047	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
2048	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
2049	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
2050	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
2051	  homomorphism is surjective.
2052	
2053	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
2054	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
2055	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
2056	  \[
2057	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
2058	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
2059	    \qquad
2060	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
2061	  \]
2062	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
2063	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
2064	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
2065	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
2066	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
2067	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
2068	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
2069	  and in particular
2070	  \[
2071	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
2072	  \]
2073	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
2074	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
2075	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
2076	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
2077	  \[
2078	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
2079	  \]
2080	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
2081	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
2082	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
2083	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
2084	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
2085	\end{proof}
2086	
2087	\section{Properness}
2088	\label{sec:gr_proper}
2089	
2090	Following Nitsure \S 1, the structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec
2091	\mathbb{Z}\) is proper. We reduce properness to the \emph{existence} half of the
2092	valuative criterion: the Mathlib criterion \texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}
2093	reduces \(\mathrm{IsProper}\,\pi\) to four ingredients --- quasi-compactness,
2094	quasi-separatedness, local finiteness of type, and the valuative criterion
2095	\(\mathrm{Existence} \sqcap \mathrm{Uniqueness}\). The first three and the
2096	uniqueness half are cheap (uniqueness is free from separatedness), so the only
2097	genuine content is \(\mathrm{Existence}\): every commutative square from a
2098	valuation ring admits a diagonal filler. This existence statement is the Nitsure
2099	chart-selection argument, which we decompose into four steps E1--E4.
2100	
2101	\subsection*{Cheap ingredients and the reduction to existence}
2102	
2103	\begin{lemma}
2104	
2105	\leanok
2106	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a compact space]
2107	  \label{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}
2108	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.compactSpace_scheme}
2109	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_glued_scheme}
2110	  The underlying topological space of the glued Grassmannian scheme
2111	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is quasi-compact: it carries the
2112	  structure of a \texttt{CompactSpace}.
2113	\end{lemma}
2114	\begin{proof}
2115	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart}
2116	  \leanok
2117	  The glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is indexed by the finite set
2118	  \(\{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\) subsets, and
2119	  each chart \(U^I = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the
2120	  \(\Spec\) of a ring, hence quasi-compact. A scheme glued from finitely many
2121	  quasi-compact charts is quasi-compact, so \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a compact
2122	  space.
2123	\end{proof}
2124	
2125	\begin{lemma}
2126	
2127	\leanok
2128	[The structure morphism is quasi-compact]
2129	  \label{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}
2130	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.quasiCompact_toSpecZ}
2131	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}
2132	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2133	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is quasi-compact.
2134	\end{lemma}
2135	\begin{proof}
2136	  \uses{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}
2137	  \leanok
2138	  The target \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is affine, so quasi-compactness of \(\pi\) is
2139	  equivalent to quasi-compactness of the total space \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), which is
2140	  a compact space by \cref{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}.
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[The structure morphism is locally of finite type]
2147	  \label{lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}
2149	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}
2150	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2151	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is locally of finite type.
2152	\end{lemma}
2153	\begin{proof}
2154	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}
2155	  \leanok
2156	  Being locally of finite type is local on the source, so it may be checked on the
2157	  chart open cover (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}). On the chart \(U^I\) the composite
2158	  \(U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \xrightarrow{\pi} \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is, by
2159	  \cref{lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}, the \(\Spec\) of the structure map \(\mathbb{Z} \to R^I
2160	  = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\). The polynomial ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is a finitely
2161	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra, so this map is of finite type; hence \(\pi\) is
2162	  locally of finite type.
2163	\end{proof}
2164	
2165	\begin{lemma}
2166	
2167	\leanok
2168	[The structure morphism is quasi-separated]
2169	  \label{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}
2170	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}
2171	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2172	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2173	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is quasi-separated.
2174	\end{lemma}
2175	\begin{proof}
2176	  \uses{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2177	  \leanok
2178	  The structure morphism is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}), and every
2179	  separated morphism is quasi-separated.
2180	\end{proof}
2181	
2182	\begin{lemma}
2183	
2184	\leanok
2185	[Uniqueness half of the valuative criterion]
2186	  \label{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2188	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2189	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2190	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{uniqueness} part of the valuative
2191	  criterion: for a valuation ring \(R\) with fraction field \(K\), any two
2192	  morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\) that agree
2193	  on the generic point \(\Spec K\) coincide.
2194	\end{lemma}
2195	\begin{proof}
2196	  \uses{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2197	  \leanok
2198	  The structure morphism is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}), and the
2199	  uniqueness half of the valuative criterion is free for any separated morphism:
2200	  two lifts agreeing on the schematically dense generic point of \(\Spec R\)
2201	  coincide because the diagonal is a (closed, in particular separated) immersion.
2202	\end{proof}
2203	
2204	\begin{lemma}
2205	
2206	\leanok
2207	[Properness from the existence half of the valuative criterion]
2208	  \label{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence}
2209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper_of_valuativeExistence}
2210	  \uses{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme, lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ,
2211	    lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ, lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ,
2212	    lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ, def:gr_to_specZ}
2213	  Suppose the structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2214	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{existence} part of the valuative
2215	  criterion. Then \(\pi\) is proper.
2216	\end{lemma}
2217	\begin{proof}
2218	  \uses{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ, lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ,
2219	    lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ, lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2220	    \leanok
2221	  The Mathlib criterion \texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion} reduces properness
2222	  of \(\pi\) to four hypotheses: \(\pi\) is quasi-compact
2223	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}), quasi-separated
2224	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}), locally of finite type
2225	  (\cref{lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}), and satisfies the full valuative
2226	  criterion. The full valuative criterion is the conjunction of its existence and
2227	  uniqueness halves; the uniqueness half holds by
2228	  \cref{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}, and the existence half is the
2229	  hypothesis. Assembling the four ingredients gives properness.
2230	\end{proof}
2231	
2232	\subsection*{The existence obligation: chart selection by minimal valuation}
2233	
2234	The existence half of the valuative criterion is the genuine geometric content,
2235	and it is exactly Nitsure's chart-selection argument. A commutative square over a
2236	valuation ring presents a valuation ring \(R\) with fraction field \(K =
2237	\operatorname{Frac}(R)\), valuation \(v := \mathrm{val}_R : K \to \Gamma\) (written
2238	multiplicatively, so \(R = \{x : v(x) \le 1\}\)), a morphism \(i_1 : \Spec K \to
2239	\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and a morphism \(i_2 : \Spec R \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) with
2240	\(i_1 \circ \pi = \Spec(R \to K) \circ i_2\); one must construct a filler
2241	\(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) making both triangles commute. We carry this out
2242	in four steps. The algebraic core --- the behaviour of the transition hom on a
2243	\(K'\)-minor --- is the following identity over the localised chart ring.
2244	
2245	\begin{lemma}
2246	
2247	\leanok
2248	[The minor-ratio identity]
2249	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}
2250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}
2251	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet,
2252	    lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities,
2253	    lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
2254	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2255	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L878-L883).
2256	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2257	  % "Now consider the homomorphism
2258	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2259	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
2260	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
2261	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
2262	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$."
2263	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2264	  For size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J, K\), the pre-localisation transition hom
2265	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) satisfies, in \(R^I_J =
2266	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), the minor-ratio identity
2267	  \[
2268	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr)\cdot P^I_J \;=\; P^I_K
2269	  \]
2270	  (where \(P^I_J, P^I_K\) are read through the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2271	  R^I_J\)). Equivalently \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) = P^I_K / P^I_J\), the
2272	  determinant of the \(K\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This generalises
2273	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul} (the case \(K = I\), where
2274	  \(P^I_I = 1\)) and is the algebraic engine of the existence argument: pulled back
2275	  through a \(K\)-point \(f\) it gives \(g(P^J_K) = f(P^I_K)/f(P^I_J)\).
2276	\end{lemma}
2277	\begin{proof}
2278	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
2279	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
2282	  \det(M_K)\) is the determinant of the \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M =
2283	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). Splitting the \(K\)-column submatrix of the product
2284	  (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and using multiplicativity of the determinant,
2285	  \(\det(M_K) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot \det(X^I_K) =
2286	  \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\cdot P^I_K\). Multiplying by \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) and
2287	  cancelling \(\det(X^I_J)^{-1}\det(X^I_J) = 1\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}) gives
2289	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K)\cdot P^I_J = P^I_K\).
2290	\end{proof}
2291	
2292	\begin{lemma}
2293	
2294	\leanok
2295	[E1 --- the \(K\)-point factors through a single chart]
2296	  \label{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization}
2297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_chart_factorization}
2298	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
2299	    lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
2300	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2301	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L868-L870).
2302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2303	  % "Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
2304	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
2305	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$."
2306	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2307	  The morphism \(i_1 : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) factors through a single
2308	  chart: there exist a size-\(d\) subset \(I\) and a ring homomorphism \(f : R^I =
2309	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\) such that
2310	  \[
2311	    i_1 \;=\; \Spec(f) \circ \iota_I : \Spec K \to U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d),
2312	  \]
2313	  where \(\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is the chart immersion of the glue
2314	  datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
2315	\end{lemma}
2316	\begin{proof}
2317	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
2318	  \leanok
2319	  Since \(K\) is a field, \(\Spec K\) is a one-point space, so its image point \(x
2320	  := i_1(\ast) \in \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a single point. The chart immersions
2321	  \(\{\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\}\) of the glue datum
2322	  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) are jointly surjective (their images cover the
2323	  glued scheme \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}), so \(x\) lies in the range of some
2324	  \(\iota_I\). Each \(\iota_I\) is an open immersion
2325	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion} gives this for the chart inclusions, and
2326	  the glue datum's immersions inherit it). A morphism out of \(\Spec K\) whose
2327	  image point lies in the range of an open immersion factors uniquely through that
2328	  open immersion; hence \(i_1 = j \circ \iota_I\) for a unique \(j : \Spec K \to
2329	  U^I\). As \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) is affine, \(j = \Spec(f)\) for a unique ring map
2330	  \(f : R^I \to K\), giving the claimed factorization.
2331	\end{proof}
2332	
2333	\begin{lemma}
2334	
2335	\leanok
2336	[E2 --- minimal-valuation chart selection]
2337	  \label{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation}
2338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_minimal_valuation}
2339	  \uses{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization, def:gr_minor_det,
2340	    lem:gr_minorDet_self}
2341	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L870-L876).
2343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2344	  % "Having fixed
2345	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
2346	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
2347	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
2348	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
2349	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$

\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Section-localization descent from a local-tilde cover (gap1 keystone, D)]
  \label{lem:section_localization_descent}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent}
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover,
    lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport, lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars_powers_algebraMap,
    lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_semilinear,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  % NOTE: BUILT axiom-clean iter-041 (isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent). This NAMED keystone is the
  % basic-open cover form \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover} instantiated at the
  % quasi-coherence cover \cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}, with the
  % per-overlap Hfr supplied by the geometric producer \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}.
  % NOTE: the general-U cover form \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover} is itself
  % axiom-clean, but its Hfr (a localization on EVERY open U ≤ D(r)) is NOT instantiable for an
  % arbitrary quasi-coherent M — a general open of the affine slice Spec R_r need not be
  % quasi-compact, so the section localization is unavailable there. The basic-open form is the one
  % gap1 uses: the sheaf-gluing engines only ever consult Hfr at basic opens D(s) and overlaps
  % D(r)⊓D(r') = D(r·r'), all quasi-compact.
  % isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation does NOT close it: that lemma needs a GLOBAL
  % presentation of M, whereas here only per-D(r) tilde data is available.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S "Sections over principal opens",
  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2152-L2170).
  % Cf. [Hartshorne], II.5.3.

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Quasi-coherent on an affine has trivial $\widetilde{(-)}$-counit (gap1)]
  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent}
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent, lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
  % NOTE: BUILT axiom-clean iter-041 (isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent). gap1 is the PRIMARY stated
  % gap of the Quot-foundations layer — the QCoh(Spec R) ≃ Mod R essential-image gap (Stacks
  % lemma-quasi-coherent-affine). The assembly is a one-liner: the genuine content lives in the
  % section-localization descent
  % (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}, keystone D), which is fed into the in-file equivalence
  % \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict} (already axiom-clean). The descent (D)
  % in turn rests on the slice-to-geometric bridge (\cref{lem:over_restrict_iso}, C) and the
  % per-element local-tilde transport (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}, P1).
  % Route: analogies/quot-gap1-transport.md.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1279--1284).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
  % be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
  % $\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to the $R$-module $\Gamma(X,
  % \mathcal{F})$."
---
  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\); restriction of scalars along the structure-sheaf
  restriction \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(s)) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\)
  makes it an \(A\)-module, and along \(R \to \Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(s)) = A\) an \(R\)-module;
  the scalar tower \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,R\,A\,\Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\) is the factorization of
  the two ring maps. The identification of the locus \(j\,''^{U}\,D(f') = D(f) \sqcap D(s)\) is
  \cref{lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[The basic-open Hfr datum from section transport (geometric producer, TOP)]
  \label{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
  \uses{lem:pullback_composite_immersion_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:composite_immersion_range_basicOpen, lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen,
    lem:gamma_image_iso_semilinear_top,
    lem:flocus_section_scalar_tower, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ, lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_iso,
    lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso, lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso,
    lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_semilinear, def:gamma_image_ring_equiv,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}
  % NOTE: BUILT axiom-clean iter-041 (section_localization_hfr_basicOpen) via the composite-immersion
  % route. Implemented as a thin wrapper over the opaque-immersion core section_localization_hfr_aux
  % (a lean_aux helper, no own block) instantiated at j = compositeBasicOpenImmersion; the planned
  % sub-lemmas (b-flocus)/(c)/(d) were absorbed inline into that core rather than built standalone (see
  % the \uses sub-lemma blocks below, whose \lean{} pins are stale — planner to split/re-pin).
  \textit{Project-bespoke: the producer manufacturing the basic-open \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) datum from the
  per-element P1 transport; the sole remaining build of \cref{lem:section_localization_descent}.}
  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\), \(D(r)\) a cover member with
  \(D(r) \le q.X_i\), and \(s : R\) with \(D(s) \le D(r)\). Then the section restriction
  \(\Gamma(M, D(s)) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\) is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\) --- i.e.\ the basic-open

The pivot is to REPLACE the proof of `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange` with a direct tilde-dictionary computation that bypasses `gstar_transpose` entirely.

**Key insight:** The STATEMENT of `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange` (lines 3322–3352) ALREADY correctly describes the tilde-transport route. The PROOF block (lines 3356–3388) is the problem — it routes through `lem:base_change_mate_section_identity` → `lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose`. The fix is a new sub-lemma + proof revision.

### Already in the chapter (no new blocks needed for these):

| Lemma | Status | Role in tilde-transport |
|---|---|---|
| `lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso` | \leanok | `(Spec φ)^* M̃ ≅ (R' ⊗_R M)~` |
| `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` | \leanok | `(Spec φ)_* M̃ ≅ (restrM)~` |
| `lem:base_change_mate_domain_read` | \leanok | `Γ(g^*(f_* M̃)) ≅ R' ⊗_R M` via Θ_src |
| `lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read` | \leanok | `Γ(f'_*(g')^* M̃) ≅ (R'⊗A)⊗_A M` via Θ_tgt |
| `lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib` | \mathlibok | `cancelBaseChange` is an iso |

### New blueprint block needed (1 lemma):

```latex
\subsection{Tilde-transport direct proof of the section-level value}
\label{sec:fbc_tilde_transport_direct}

% This subsection replaces the conjugate-calculus proof route for
% lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange.
% It does NOT depend on lem:base_change_mate_section_identity or
% lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose.

\begin{lemma}
  [Direct section-level value of the affine base-change map]
  \label{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_sections_direct}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_base_change_mate_sections_direct}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map,
        lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,
        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
  In the affine-affine model --- S = Spec R, S' = Spec R', X = Spec A,
  F = M̃ for an A-module M, g = Spec ψ (ψ : R → R'), f = Spec φ (φ : R → A) ---
  the composition
  \[
    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
      \;\circ\; \Gamma\!\bigl(\alpha\bigr)
      \;\circ\; \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
    \;:\; R' \otimes_R M
    \;\longrightarrow\; (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
  \]
  equals the inverse cancellation isomorphism
  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1} : r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\)
  of Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}, where
  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are the domain and codomain
  identifications of Lemmas~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}
  and~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map,
        lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,
        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
  The proof is a direct module computation. The map
  \(\alpha = \mathtt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) is defined as the natural
  transformation obtained from the adjunction
  \((f'^*, f'_*)\) and \((g^*, g_*)\) applied to the identity on \(f_* \widetilde M\).
  Concretely, on an affine chart the adjunction units/counits reduce to algebra maps
  between tensor products. Tracing \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) through its definition yields,
  on a generator \(r' \otimes m\):
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Apply \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\): pass to the \(\Gamma\)-representation
      of the domain via the two tilde dictionaries
      (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}).
    \item Apply \(\Gamma(\alpha)\): use the explicit definition of
      \(\mathtt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) as an adjunction natural transformation;
      on global sections over the affine model this collapses to the canonical map
      \(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) sending
      \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\) (the universal property of the
      tensor product and the algebra map \(\varphi : R \to A\)).
    \item Apply \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\): the result lands in
      \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
  \end{enumerate}
  The composed map is \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m =
  \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}(r' \otimes m)\). Since
  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}),
  so is \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\). No flatness hypothesis is needed.
\end{proof}
```

### Revised proof for `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange`:

The existing STATEMENT block (lines 3283–3354) is correct and unchanged. The PROOF block (lines 3356–3388) is REPLACED with:

```latex
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map,
        lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,
        lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_sections_direct,
        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_sections_direct}, the composition
  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\)
  equals \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\), which is an isomorphism
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). Hence \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an
  isomorphism (being the composition of isomorphisms
  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}} \circ \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1} \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{-1}\)).
  Since both source and target of \(\alpha\) are quasi-coherent, the tilde-equivalence
  is fully faithful and \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\Gamma(\alpha) \Rightarrow \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha\).
  No flatness hypothesis is needed; the identification is an \(R'\)-module isomorphism.
\end{proof}
```

### Updated `\uses{}` seam for `lem:affine_base_change_pushforward`:

Remove `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange`'s dependency on `lem:base_change_mate_section_identity` and `lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose`. The revised `\uses{}` for the entire assembly:

```latex
      lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange,
      lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
      lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
```

`lem:base_change_mate_section_identity` and `lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose` are **removed from the dependency chain** of `lem:affine_base_change_pushforward`. They remain in the chapter as dead-end sorry nodes (the conjugate-calculus scaffolding stays for the record but is no longer on the critical path).
